response:
**Results**

We evaluated ten experimental variants of the proposed method on the validation set, reporting mean accuracy and standard deviation over five random seeds for each configuration (Table 1). Variant_03, which omits the proposed component and serves as our baseline, achieved a mean validation accuracy of 67.8 ± 0.6. Of the nine variants incorporating the proposed component, only variant_08 surpassed baseline performance, achieving 70.4 ± 0.5 accuracy—an absolute improvement of +2.6 percentage points over the baseline.

The remaining eight variants did not outperform the baseline. Specifically, variant_01 achieved 67.2 ± 0.8, variant_02 66.9 ± 0.7, variant_04 65.1 ± 0.9, variant_05 66.3 ± 1.0, variant_06 64.7 ± 0.7, variant_07 65.8 ± 0.8, variant_09 66.5 ± 0.7, and variant_10 65.9 ± 0.8. These configurations exhibited accuracies ranging from 64.7 to 67.2, all falling short of the baseline. Standard deviations across all experiments were modest (0.5–1.0), indicating consistent performance within each variant.

Taken together, these results suggest that the proposed component does not uniformly improve validation accuracy across configurations. Notably, only variant_08 demonstrates a substantial and consistent gain over the baseline, while the other variants underperform. This indicates that the benefit of the proposed method is highly sensitive to specific configuration choices. Further analysis is warranted to elucidate the factors that enable variant_08 to outperform both the baseline and the other experimental configurations.
